query: The Things Network EU868 duty cycle EIRP LoRa frequency plan

results.title: EU863-870 MHz Band | The Things Network
results.description: The LoRa Alliance® recommends a duty cycle limitation of 1% in the European band which means that a device can transmit for no more than 1% of the time while ensuring the maximum EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power) of +16 dBm.
results.content:
The LoRa Alliance® recommends a duty cycle limitation of 1% in the European band which means that a device can transmit for no more than 1% of the time while ensuring the maximum EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power) of +16 dBm.
All EU868-870 end devices must support one of the following three data rate options. DR0 – DR5 – the minimal data rate set supported to obtain the LoRaWAN certification. ... The Effective Isotropic Radiated Power (EIRP) is the total power radiated by an isotropic antenna in a single direction.

The maximum application payload size is calculated using M-8 if the FOpts field is absent. The table below shows both the maximum MACPayload size and the application payload size for each Data Rate, and it is compatible with repeaters. The LoRa Alliance® recommends a duty cycle limitation of 1% in the European band which means that a device can transmit for no more than 1% of the time while ensuring the maximum EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power) of +16 dBm.

results.title: Regional Parameters | The Things Network
results.description: For example, in the Netherlands (NL), both EU868-870 and EU433 can be used. The regional parameters include physical layer parameters such as frequency plans (channel plans), mandatory channel frequencies and data rates for join-request messages. The Regional Parameters also include LoRaWAN layer parameters such as maximum payload size.
results.content:
For example, in the Netherlands (NL), both EU868-870 and EU433 can be used. The regional parameters include physical layer parameters such as frequency plans (channel plans), mandatory channel frequencies and data rates for join-request messages. The Regional Parameters also include LoRaWAN layer parameters such as maximum payload size.
LoRaWAN operates in unlicensed radio spectrum. This means that anyone can use the radio frequencies without having to pay million dollar fees for transmission rights. It is similar to WiFi, which uses the 2.4GHz and 5GHz ISM bands worldwide.
LoRaWAN uses lower radio frequencies with a longer range. The fact that frequencies have a longer range also comes with more restrictions, that are often country-specific. This poses a challenge for LoRaWAN, as it strives to maintain uniformity across various regions of the world.
These LoRaWAN regional specifications do not specify everything either. They only cover a region by specifying the common denominator. For example, the LoRaWAN regional parameters for Asia only specify a common subset of channels - but there are variations between regulations in Asian countries.

results.title: Frequency Plans | The Things Network
results.description: This is a list of frequency plan definitions used in The Things Network. These frequency plans are based on what is specified in the LoRaWAN regional parameters document.
results.content: This is a list of frequency plan definitions used in The Things Network. These frequency plans are based on what is specified in the LoRaWAN regional parameters document.

results.title: LoRaWAN Frequency Bands in Europe: A Guide to EU868 Regulations and Compliance | EdgePilot.io
results.description: The sub-bands relevant to LoRaWAN are: ERP = Effective Radiated Power. The LoRaWAN specification uses EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power), where the maximum is +16 dBm (40 mW).
results.content:
The sub-bands relevant to LoRaWAN are: ERP = Effective Radiated Power. The LoRaWAN specification uses EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power), where the maximum is +16 dBm (40 mW).
The EU868 band is divided into several sub-bands, each with its own maximum transmit power and duty cycle limitations. This is fundamentally different from regions like the US (US915), where a wider frequency band is available with different rules. ETSI EN 300 220-2 divides the 863–870 MHz range into six sub-bands, each designated by a letter. The sub-bands relevant to LoRaWAN are: ERP = Effective Radiated Power. The LoRaWAN specification uses EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power), where the maximum is +16 dBm (40 mW).
EIRP ≈ ERP + 2.15 dBi antenna gain. The M sub-band (868.0–868.6 MHz) is where the three mandatory LoRaWAN uplink channels sit: 868.1, 868.3, and 868.5 MHz. Every LoRaWAN end device must implement these channels, and all gateways must listen on them. The five additional channels at 867.1–867.9 MHz fall within the L sub-band (1% duty cycle).
Note that the K sub-band (863–865 MHz) has a much stricter 0.1% duty cycle, which limits its usefulness for LoRaWAN traffic. While only three channels are mandatory, a standard EU868 deployment uses eight LoRa channels plus one FSK channel.
→LoRaWAN in Europe uses the EU868 band (863–870 MHz), divided into six ETSI sub-bands (K, L, M, N, P, Q) with different power and duty cycle limits.

results.title: Duty Cycle | The Things Network
results.description: On The Things Network’s The Things Stack Sandbox a Fair Use Policy applies which limits the uplink airtime to 30 seconds per day (24 hours) per node and the downlink messages to 10 messages per day (24 hours) per node.
results.content:
On The Things Network’s The Things Stack Sandbox a Fair Use Policy applies which limits the uplink airtime to 30 seconds per day (24 hours) per node and the downlink messages to 10 messages per day (24 hours) per node.
Additionally, the LoRaWAN specification specifies duty cycles for the join frequencies, which are used for over-the-air activations (OTAA) by every LoRaWAN-compliant end device.
On The Things Network’s The Things Stack Sandbox a Fair Use Policy applies which limits the uplink airtime to 30 seconds per day (24 hours) per node and the downlink messages to 10 messages per day (24 hours) per node. If you use a private network, these limits do not apply, but you still have to be compliant with the governmental and LoRaWAN limits.

results.title: Regional Limitations of RF Use in LoRaWAN | The Things Network
results.description: The LoRa Alliance® recommends a duty cycle limitation of 1% in the European band which means that a device can transmit for no more than 1% of the time while ensuring the maximum EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power) of +16 dBm.
results.content:
The LoRa Alliance® recommends a duty cycle limitation of 1% in the European band which means that a device can transmit for no more than 1% of the time while ensuring the maximum EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power) of +16 dBm.
For the US ISM band, the dwell time for transmitting a LoRaWAN message must not exceed 400 ms. It is important to consider this dwell time limitation when designing and operating devices. For example, if a device is transmitting data at a high rate, it may need to use a faster hopping rate in order to comply with the 400 ms dwell time limitation. Both end devices and gateways are also limited to a maximum EIRP of +30 dBm.
The EIRP of 16 dBm is equal to 40 mW and is equivalent to an ERP of 14 dBm, which is equal to 25 mW. To learn more about EIRP and ERP, you can refer to the LoRaWAN® section.
Usually, the limitations of sub-bands K, L, M, N and Q are applied for uplink transmissions and the limitations of sub-band P are mainly applied for downlink transmissions. Usually, a number of end devices in a LoRaWAN network is way higher than a number of gateways.

The Things Stack uses the github.com/TheThingsNetwork/lorawan-frequency-plans as default source for fetching frequency plans. Therefore, contributing to this open source repository makes frequency plans automatically available to Stack deployments with default settings.
Frequency plans are defined for a band. Bands are specified by the LoRa Alliance Technical Committee and are published as the LoRaWAN Regional Parameters technical document.

results.title: What Is LoRa Frequency Planning? Regional Bands (EU868, US915, AS923)
results.description: This band operates within the 863-870 MHz range, and it is regulated by ETSI (European Telecommunications Standards Institute). The EU868 band is divided into several sub-bands, each with specific characteristics and limitations regarding duty cycle and transmit power.
results.content:
This band operates within the 863-870 MHz range, and it is regulated by ETSI (European Telecommunications Standards Institute). The EU868 band is divided into several sub-bands, each with specific characteristics and limitations regarding duty cycle and transmit power.
EU868: The European Band In Europe, the EU868 band is commonly used for LoRa communication. This band operates within the 863-870 MHz range, and it is regulated by ETSI (European Telecommunications Standards Institute). The EU868 band is divided into several sub-bands, each with specific characteristics and limitations regarding duty cycle and transmit power.
AS923's dynamic nature makes it suitable for countries with diverse regulatory landscapes, and it is often used in applications such as logistics and smart metering. Challenges in Frequency Planning Despite the advantages of LoRa technology, frequency planning comes with its own set of challenges.
Network designers must also consider factors like the density of IoT devices, geographical coverage, and environmental conditions to achieve efficient frequency planning. Optimizing Network Design To optimize LoRa frequency planning, network designers must conduct thorough assessments of the area in which the network will operate.
Techniques such as frequency hopping, adaptive data rate management, and use of advanced antenna systems can enhance network performance and reliability. The Future of LoRa Frequency Planning As IoT continues to grow, the need for effective LoRa frequency planning becomes increasingly important.

results.description: Are the EIRP TX levels listed here correct? https://www.thethingsnetwork.org/docs/lorawan/regional-parameters/eu868/ They flow highest to lowest vs. lowest to highest in the LoRa Alliance RP’s and other documentation. Thanks in advance

data.question: Are the EIRP TX levels listed here correct? https://www.thethingsnetwork.org/docs/lorawan/regional-parameters/eu868/ They flow highest to lowest vs. lowest to highest in the LoRa Alliance RP’s and other documentation. Thanks in advance

results.title: 1% Duty Cycle Regulation in Europe
results.description: 1% is for all devices in this frequency band. Whatever where or how you emit it.

data.title: 1% Duty Cycle Regulation in Europe
data.question:
Hello,

I'd like to develop some applications in the area of wildlife conservation and came across LoRa as an option for long range communication on areas where there is very limited infrastructure.

But there are two things I'd like to know about the 1% regulation in Europe:

a) does the 1% regulation also applies in fairly remote areas (for example inside a forest far away from the next city)?

b) does the 1% limitation only applies when using the LoRaWAN protocol? in other words: would "plain" LoRa transmission be exempt from this regulation?
data.top_comment: 1% is for all devices in this frequency band. Whatever where or how you emit it.
